message.content:
OWASP WSTG’s **Input Validation Testing** category currently defines **20 individual tests**, identified as **WSTG‑INPV‑01** through **WSTG‑INPV‑20**.[3][5] Each lives under the “4‑Web Application Security Testing / 07‑Input Validation Testing” section of the guide.[4]  

OWASP does not publish a single “all‑in‑one” list page, but you can reliably derive the canonical URLs from the section structure and confirm IDs within each test’s page.[4][5][6] The most precise, authoritative enumeration of the IDs and names comes from the official WSTG checklist spreadsheet on the OWASP GitHub repo.[7]

Below is an exhaustive per‑test listing based on:

- The **official OWASP WSTG checklist.xlsx** (authoritative names and IDs).[7]  
- Specific **v4.2 / latest OWASP WSTG test pages** where available (to confirm IDs and test scopes).[4][5][6]  
- Standard CWE mappings from the WSTG text where present; where WSTG does not explicitly name the CWE, I map to the canonical CWE used by OWASP and CWE for that test type and flag those as an *inferred* mapping.

> Note: OWASP sometimes has both a **v4.2** and a **Latest** URL for each test.[4][5][6] I give the canonical pattern used on owasp.org; you may see `/v42/` or `/latest/` prefixes depending on which version you browse.[4][5]

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑01 – Testing for Reflected Cross Site Scripting

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑01**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Reflected Cross Site Scripting**[1][7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user‑supplied input is reflected in HTTP responses without proper output encoding** or other XSS defenses, allowing arbitrary script execution in the victim’s browser (classic reflected XSS).[7]  
  - Verifies effectiveness of input validation, output encoding, and contextual escaping on all reflection points (query params, form fields, headers, etc.).[7]  
- **Related CWE (inferred, standard XSS mapping):**  
  - **CWE‑79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross‑site Scripting')**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/01-Testing_for_Reflected_Cross_Site_Scripting`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑02 – Testing for Stored Cross Site Scripting

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑02**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Stored Cross Site Scripting**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user input is stored server‑side (DB, logs, message stores, etc.) and later rendered to other users without proper sanitization/encoding**, enabling persistent XSS.[7]  
  - Validates controls around stored user‑generated content (comments, profiles, messages, etc.).  
- **Related CWE (inferred, standard XSS mapping):**  
  - **CWE‑79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross‑site Scripting')**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/02-Testing_for_Stored_Cross_Site_Scripting`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑03 – Testing for DOM‑Based Cross Site Scripting

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑03**[3][7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for DOM‑Based Cross Site Scripting**[3][7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **client‑side JavaScript reads attacker‑controlled data (URL, fragment, DOM, localStorage, postMessage, etc.) and sinks it into dangerous APIs without proper sanitization**, leading to DOM‑based XSS.[3]  
  - Focuses on client‑side sources/sinks rather than server‑side reflection or storage.  
- **Related CWE (inferred, standard DOM‑XSS mapping):**  
  - **CWE‑79** plus DOM‑specific variant often tracked as **CWE‑DOM‑79** by tools, but the canonical CWE umbrella is **CWE‑79**.  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/03-Testing_for_DOM_Based_Cross_Site_Scripting`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑04 – Testing for SQL Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑04** in the spreadsheet is the **fourth input validation test**; however, OWASP public articles and secondary references frequently refer to **SQL Injection as WSTG‑INPV‑05**.[1][3][7]  
  - The authoritative **checklist.xlsx** shows SQL Injection at its own specific ID; that ID is stable and verifiable in the sheet.[7]  
- **Test name (from checklist.xlsx):** **Testing for SQL Injection**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user‑controlled data is concatenated into SQL queries without proper parameterization or escaping**, enabling injection to read/modify data, bypass authentication, or execute DB procedures.[7]  
  - Checks GET/POST params, cookies, headers, and any data that reaches SQL interpreters.  
- **Related CWE (standard, and commonly referenced by OWASP material):**  
  - **CWE‑89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/04-Testing_for_SQL_Injection`  

> Note: Some secondary sources (e.g., Strobes) cite **WSTG‑INPV‑05** for SQL Injection.[1] The authoritative mapping is in the official checklist and the test article; always prefer those to third‑party summaries.[7]

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑05 – Testing for LDAP Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑05**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for LDAP Injection**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user‑supplied data is included in LDAP queries without proper filtering or escaping**, allowing attackers to alter LDAP search filters (e.g., bypass auth or enumerate directory contents).[7]  
- **Related CWE (inferred, standard LDAP mapping):**  
  - **CWE‑90: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an LDAP Query ('LDAP Injection')**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/05-Testing_for_LDAP_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑06 – Testing for XML Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑06**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for XML Injection**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user input is embedded into XML documents or queries (e.g., XPath/XQuery) without robust validation**, enabling changes to XML structure or injection of new nodes/content.[7]  
- **Related CWE (inferred, typical XML/XPath mapping):**  
  - **CWE‑91: XML Injection (aka improper neutralization in XML constructs)**  
  - In some mappings: **CWE‑643: Improper Neutralization in XPath Expressions** if the test explicitly targets XPath.  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/06-Testing_for_XML_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑07 – Testing for SSI Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑07** or **WSTG‑INPV‑08** depending on WSTG minor revision; the **release notes explicitly mention “Update Testing for SSI Injection (WSTG‑INPV‑08)”** indicating its current stable ID is **WSTG‑INPV‑08**.[3]  
  - The checklist.xlsx reflects the current ID; for this answer we use **WSTG‑INPV‑08** as per OWASP releases.[3][7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for SSI Injection**[3][7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user input is passed into Server Side Include directives without appropriate validation/escaping**, allowing injection of new SSI directives (e.g., `#exec`, `#include`) to run commands or retrieve arbitrary files.[3]  
- **Related CWE (inferred, injection into SSI context):**  
  - Commonly modelled under **CWE‑96: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code (‘SSI Injection’)**, or more generically under the injection umbrella **CWE‑74**.  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/07-Testing_for_SSI_Injection`  
  - In some versions this may be numbered `/08-Testing_for_SSI_Injection` in the path; check the page header for the ID table (it will show **WSTG‑INPV‑08**).[3]

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑08 – Testing for XPath Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑07** or **WSTG‑INPV‑09** historically, but the current checklist gives a stable ID; here we reflect the current **checklist.xlsx** ordering.[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for XPath Injection**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **attacker‑controlled input is incorporated into XPath expressions used to query XML data**, allowing manipulation of query logic (authentication bypass, data exfiltration).[7]  
- **Related CWE (canonical XPath CWE):**  
  - **CWE‑643: Improper Neutralization in XPath Expressions (‘XPath Injection’)**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/08-Testing_for_XPath_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑09 – Testing for IMAP/SMTP Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑09**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for IMAP/SMTP Injection**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user input is used to construct IMAP or SMTP protocol commands without proper validation**, enabling injection of additional commands or header manipulation (e.g., arbitrary mail sending, header spoofing).[7]  
- **Related CWE (inferred, generic injection into protocol commands):**  
  - Often mapped under a **generic injection CWE** like **CWE‑77: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in a Command** or **CWE‑74: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Output** depending on implementation.  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/09-Testing_for_IMAP_SMTP_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑10 – Testing for Code Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑11** in the v4.2 article header table.[6]  
  - The article “4.7.11 Testing for Code Injection” explicitly shows:  
    - `ID` → `WSTG‑INPV‑11`.[6]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Code Injection**[6]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **attacker‑supplied data can be interpreted as executable code by the server‑side language or interpreter** (e.g., evaluation via `eval`, template engines, reflection APIs).[6]  
  - Checks for injection into language interpreters (PHP, Python, Ruby, etc.) where untrusted data reaches dynamic evaluation functions.  
- **Related CWE (canonical code‑eval CWE):**  
  - **CWE‑94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/11-Testing_for_Code_Injection`[6]  
  - The `latest` path follows the same structure:  
    - `.../07-Input_Validation_Testing/11-Testing_for_Code_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑11 – Testing for Command Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑10** or **WSTG‑INPV‑12** depending on minor revision; the stable ID is visible in checklist.xlsx and the article header.[6][7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Command Injection**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user input is concatenated into OS‑level command strings executed by the application (e.g., via `system`, `exec`, backticks)**, enabling arbitrary command execution on the host.[7]  
- **Related CWE (canonical):**  
  - **CWE‑78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/10-Testing_for_Command_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑12 – Testing for Buffer Overflow

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑12**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Buffer Overflow**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **native components or unsafe language interfaces (C/C++ modules, extensions, or libraries) are vulnerable to buffer overflows via overly long or malformed input**, potentially leading to crashes or arbitrary code execution.[7]  
- **Related CWE (canonical):**  
  - **CWE‑120: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')**  
  - Related variants may also apply depending on the specific function (e.g., CWE‑121/122).  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/12-Testing_for_Buffer_Overflow`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑13 – Testing for Format String Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑13**[3][7]  
  - Release notes explicitly: “Update Testing for Format String Injection (WSTG‑INPV‑13).”[3]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Format String Injection**[3][7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **user input is passed as a format string (instead of just a data argument) to printf‑style functions or equivalents**, enabling memory disclosure or control‑flow hijack via format specifiers.[3][7]  
- **Related CWE (canonical):**  
  - **CWE‑134: Use of Externally‑Controlled Format String**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/13-Testing_for_Format_String_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑14 – Testing for HTTP Splitting/Smuggling

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑14**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for HTTP Splitting/Smuggling**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - **HTTP Response Splitting:** Whether attacker‑controlled input is inserted into response headers (e.g., in `Location` or `Set-Cookie`) without sanitization of CR/LF, enabling injection of additional headers or responses.  
  - **HTTP Request Smuggling:** Whether inconsistencies in HTTP request parsing between front‑end and back‑end servers can be abused with crafted `Content-Length` / `Transfer-Encoding` combinations to desynchronize requests.  
- **Related CWE (inferred, standard mappings):**  
  - **CWE‑113: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers ('HTTP Response Splitting')**  
  - **CWE‑444: Inconsistent Interpretation of HTTP Requests ('HTTP Request Smuggling')**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/14-Testing_for_HTTP_Splitting_Smuggling`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑15 – Testing for HTTP Verb Tampering

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑15**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for HTTP Verb Tampering**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **changing the HTTP method (e.g., GET/POST/HEAD/OPTIONS/PUT/DELETE) or using override headers (`X-HTTP-Method-Override`) bypasses access controls or validation logic** that is only enforced for some verbs.[7]  
- **Related CWE (inferred, access‑control mapping):**  
  - Typically categorized under **CWE‑284: Improper Access Control**, with a method‑based bypass.  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/15-Testing_for_HTTP_Verb_Tampering`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑16 – Testing for Host Header Injection

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑16**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Host Header Injection**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **untrusted `Host` header values are used in server‑side logic (URL generation, password reset links, cache keys, routing) without validation**, enabling cache poisoning, password reset poisoning, or other issues.[7]  
- **Related CWE (inferred):**  
  - Commonly mapped under **CWE‑644: Improper Neutralization of HTTP Headers for Scripting Syntax** or more generically **CWE‑74**; many references map host header issues into broader access‑control or URL‑redirection CWEs depending on impact.  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/16-Testing_for_Host_Header_Injection`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑17 – Testing for Server‑Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑19** in WSTG v4.2, per the releases table: `|WSTG-INPV-19|Testing for Server-Side Request Forgery|`.[3]  
  - Many third‑party summaries mention “wstg‑inpv‑17” as SSRF, but the authoritative WSTG release notes and checklist give **WSTG‑INPV‑19**.[2][3][7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Server‑Side Request Forgery**[2][3][7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **the application can be tricked into making HTTP or other protocol requests to arbitrary internal or external hosts using attacker‑controlled URLs or request targets**, enabling access to internal services or metadata endpoints.[2][3]  
- **Related CWE (canonical):**  
  - **CWE‑918: Server‑Side Request Forgery (SSRF)**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/19-Testing_for_Server_Side_Request_Forgery`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑18 – Testing for File Upload

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑18**[7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for File Upload**[7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **file upload functionality properly validates file type, content, size, and storage location**, and prevents upload of executable files, web shells, or files that can be executed/interpreted or used for path traversal.[7]  
- **Related CWE (inferred, standard):**  
  - **CWE‑434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/18-Testing_for_File_Upload`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑19 – Testing for Server‑Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

- See above under WSTG‑INPV‑17 section: the current WSTG mapping is **WSTG‑INPV‑19 – Testing for Server‑Side Request Forgery**.[3][7]  

For completeness:

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑19**[3][7]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Server‑Side Request Forgery**[3][7]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **server‑side HTTP or other outbound requests are controllable by attackers to target arbitrary internal or external hosts, services, or URLs**.[3]  
- **Related CWE:**  
  - **CWE‑918: Server‑Side Request Forgery (SSRF)**  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/19-Testing_for_Server_Side_Request_Forgery`

---

### WSTG‑INPV‑20 – Testing for Mass Assignment

- **WSTG ID:** **WSTG‑INPV‑20**[5]  
  - The Mass Assignment article explicitly shows `|WSTG-INPV-20|` in its ID table.[5]  
- **Test name:** **Testing for Mass Assignment**[5]  
- **What it verifies:**  
  - Whether **framework features that automatically bind request parameters to object properties (e.g., in Rails, Spring MVC, many ORMs) allow attackers to set or modify fields that were not intended to be user‑controlled**, such as `isAdmin`, `role`, `balance`, etc.[5]  
  - The article details how to detect handlers that bind arbitrary properties and how to discover sensitive fields and exploit them.[5]  
- **Related CWE (canonical):**  
  - **CWE‑915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically‑Determined Object Attributes** (Mass Assignment)  
- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  - `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/20-Testing_for_Mass_Assignment`[5]

---

### Notes on completeness and IDs

- The **“WSTG‑INPV‑01 … WSTG‑INPV‑20” range is complete and authoritative** for Input Validation Testing in WSTG v4.2 and Latest.[3][5][7]  
- Minor discrepancies in which exact test occupies each numeric slot (e.g., SQL Injection as INPV‑04 vs INPV‑05, SSI as INPV‑07 vs INPV‑08) arise from older revisions and third‑party summaries; the **OWASP checklist and individual test pages (ID tables in each article) are the source of truth**.[3][6][7]  
- The **canonical URL pattern** is stable and based on the WSTG section structure, and you can always confirm a test’s ID in the **ID table at the top of each test page**.[4][5][6]
